context.description: Reflective prose on the search for meaning and the limits of language, contrasting magic, religion, and science as ways of approaching mystery. It argues that religions borrow from one another and fragment into distinctive vocabularies, while shared perceptual constants remain available to everyone. A shared example of the sun is introduced to support the claim that universal understanding does not belong exclusively to scientific method.
normalization_notes: Source page is German philosophical prose; translated into English control-language summary.
Visible text is a continuation fragment without a chapter heading.
The final sentence is truncated in the source excerpt.

rows.paragraph_text: The text contrasts different ways of approaching mystery, including magic, religion, and science, and questions the human drive to impose will on reality.
rows.text_content: Approaches to mystery through magic, religion, and science.

rows.paragraph_text: It describes how religions borrow from one another, split into distinct traditions, and develop their own terms, meanings, rules, and myths to assert exclusivity.
rows.text_content: Religious traditions fragment into distinct vocabularies and identities.

rows.paragraph_text: The author laments linguistic barriers and argues that universal constants of perception are available to everyone, not only through the scientific method.
rows.text_content: Universal constants of perception are not exclusive to science.

rows.paragraph_text: A shared example begins with the sun as a universally recognized warm, bright object, introducing a common-perception argument.
rows.text_content: The sun as an example of shared perception.
